Its county seat is Decaturville.

This county is named after naval hero Stephen Decatur, Jr., who gained national recognition in the First Barbary War and the Second Barbary War and the War of 1812 by his leadership and achievements at sea.

The county was created in November 1845 from the part of Perry County west of the Tennessee River in response to a petition by citizens on the west side of the river who lacked easy access to the county seat, which was located on the east side of the river.

In 2015, the Decatur County clerk of court and the entire staff of that office resigned, to express conscientious objection to the United States Supreme Court s ruling in Obergefell v. Hodges , which would oblige the office to issue marriage licenses to same-sex couples.

Jake Burton Carpenter, founder of Burton Snowboards, perfected snowboard design in his garage in Manchester.
The company operated out of Manchester until 1992 when it relocated to Burlington, Vermont.
The town has three distinct state-recognized historic districts the Depot district located on Highland Avenue and Elm Street, Bonnet Street, just north of Main Street, and Main Street itself.
Manchester is located in north-central Bennington County, lying between the Green Mountains to the east and the Taconic Range to the west.
Equinox Mountain, the highest summit in the Taconics, with an elevation of , is in the western part of the town.
Manchester is drained by the Batten Kill, Lye Brook, Munson Brook, Bromley Brook, and Bourn Brook.
The Lye Brook Falls Hiking Trail which leads to one of the highest waterfalls in Vermont is a popular local attraction.

s_text: census-designated place (CDP) in Suffolk County, New York, United States.
The population was 2,838 at the 2010 census.
Moriches is served by the William Floyd School District.
The name Moriches comes from Meritces, a Native American who owned land on Moriches Neck.
One of the community s most famous businesses landmarks was the Jurgielewicz Duck Farm founded in 1919 on the edge of the Forge River.
At its peak the farm located on the edge of the Montauk Branch of the Long Island Rail Road claimed to be America s largest free-range duck operation, raising 1 million Pekin ducks a year.
In 2011, it declared bankruptcy, and it closed in August 2011.
Moriches is located at (40.802846, -72.822444).
According to the United States Census Bureau, the CDP has a total area of , of which is land and , or 9.99 , is water.
As of the census of 2000, there were 2,319 people, 1,116 households, and 669 families residing in the CDP.

Moriches wird vom William Floyd School District bedient.
Der Name Moriches stammt von Meritces, einem amerikanischen Ureinwohner, der Land auf Moriches Neck besaß.
Einer der bekanntesten Wirtschaftsmarkte der Gemeinde war die 1919 am Rande des Forge River gegründete Jurgielewicz Duck Farm.

Auf seinem Höhepunkt behauptete die Farm am Rande der Montauk-Abteilung der Long Island Rail Road, die größte Freiraum-Entenbetrieb in Amerika zu sein, wobei 1 Million Pekin-Enten pro Jahr gezüchtet wurden.

s_text: Final Fight , which is an archetypal side scrolling beat-em-up game.

Player one controls Paul the blond-haired with balanced fighting skills, and Bobby the tough, but slow break-dancer from left to right through each level (most of which are split into three or more scenes), fighting with the enemy characters who appear, until they reach a confrontation with a stronger boss character at the end of the level.
Once that boss is beaten, the players automatically move on to the next stage.
Enemies appear from both sides of the screen, and the players must defeat all of them to progress.
If the players try to simply travel through the levels without fighting, the screen will stop scrolling until all current enemies have been defeated, before allowing the players to continue progress.
Enemies may move outside the confines of the screen, but players may not
t_text: Final Fight , which is an archetypal side scrolling beat-em-up game.

Up to four player characters move from left to right through each level (most of which are split into 3 or more scenes), fighting with the enemy characters who appear, until they reach a confrontation with a stronger boss character at the end of the level.
Once that boss is beaten, the players automatically move on to the next stage.
Enemies appear from both sides of the screen and from out of doorways or entrances set into the background, and the player(s) must defeat all of them to progress.
If the players try to simply travel through the levels without fighting, the screen will stop scrolling until all current enemies have been defeated, before allowing the players to continue progress.
Enemies may move outside the confines of the screen, but players may no
translated_text: Final Fight, qui est un jeu de battement par le côté archétypique.

Le joueur un contrôle Paul, le blond aux cheveux roux avec des compétences de combat équilibrées, et Bobby, le dur, mais lent break-dancer de gauche à droite à travers chaque niveau (la plupart desquels sont divisés en trois scènes ou plus), se battant avec les personnages ennemis qui apparaissent, jusqu'à ce qu'ils atteignent une confrontation avec un personnage patron plus fort à la fin du niveau.
Une fois que le patron est battu, les joueurs passent automatiquement à l'étape suivante.
Des ennemis apparaissent des deux côtés de l'écran, et les joueurs doivent les vaincre tous pour progresser.

Si les joueurs essaient de simplement traverser les niveaux sans se battre, l'écran cessera de défiler jusqu'à ce que tous les ennemis actuels aient été vaincus, avant de permettre aux joueurs de continuer à progresser.

Au recensement de 2000, il y avait 2 914 personnes, 1 125 ménages et 774 familles résidant dans la CDP.
La densité de population était de 384,0 km (994,1 mi).
Il y avait 1 272 logements à une densité moyenne de 167,6 km (433,9 mi).
La composition raciale du CDP était de 96,64 Blancs, 0,58 Afro-Américains, 0,27 Amérindiens, 0,58 Asiatiques, 0,03 Insulaires du Pacifique, 0,69 d'autres races et 1,20 de deux races ou plus.
Les Hispaniques ou les Latinos de toute race représentaient 1,65 de la population.

Il y avait 1.125 ménages, dont 33.6 avaient des enfants de moins de 18 ans vivant avec eux, 54.4 étaient des couples mariés vivant ensemble, 8.5 avaient une femme qui ne présentait pas de mari et 31.2 étaient des non-familles.
22,0 des ménages étaient composés de personnes et 5,8 avaient une personne seule âgée de 65 ans ou plus.
La taille moyenne du ménage était de 2,59 et la taille moyenne de la famille de 3,05.
Dans le CDP, la population était répartie avec 26,3 personnes âgées de moins de 18 ans, 7,6 de 18 à 24, 34,1 de 25 à 44, 23,2 de 45 à 64, et 8,8 de 65 ans ou plus.
L'âge moyen était de 36 ans.

s_text: United States.
The population was 1,659 at the 2010 census.
The Town of Minetto is located south of the City of Oswego, which it borders.
Minetto was once a company town of the now-closed Columbia Mills textile factory.

According to the United States Census Bureau, the town has a total area of 6.1 square miles (15.7 km ), of which, 5.8 square miles (15.0 km ) of it is land and 0.3 square miles (0.7 km ) of it (4.30 ) is water.
The town is situated along the Oswego River and Oswego Canal.
As of the census of 2000, there were 1,663 people, 635 households, and 499 families residing in the town.
The population density was 287.1 people per square mile (110.9 km ).
There were 660 housing units at an average density of 114.0 per square mile (44.0 km ).
The racial makeup of the town was 97.78 White, 0.24 Black or African American, 0.12 Native American, 0.66 Asian, 0.48 from other races, and 0.72 from two or more races.
Hispanic or Latino of any race were 1.20 of the population.

There were 635 households out of which 34.5 had children under the age of 18 living with them, 66.5 were married couples living together, 9.3 had a female householder with no husband present, and 21.4 were non-families.
17.3 of all households were made up of individuals and 7.9 had someone living alone who was 65 years of age or older.
The average household size was 2.62 and the average family size was 2.95.
In the town the population was spread out with 25.1 under the age of 18, 6.9 from 18 to 24, 26.4 from 25 to 44, 29.8 from 45 to 64, and 11.8 who were 65 years of age or older.

The population was 356 as of the 2010 census.
Grampian is located in western Clearfield County at (40.965240, -78.611294).
U.S. Route 219 passes through the borough, leading northwest to DuBois and southwest to Mahaffey.
Pennsylvania Route 879 leads east to Curwensville and to Clearfield, the county seat.
According to the United States Census Bureau, the borough has a total area of , all of it land.
The town of Grampian is the remnants of a booming coal industry.
The town had reached such sustainable population that a municipal water supply was incorporated.
The water supply is piped from Curwensville and then dispersed into several water tanks for community use.
The water authority board handles budgets, grants and hiring of licensed technicians.
Grampian Penn Township Water Authority employs one full-time licensed technician to run day-to-day operations.
As of the census of 2000, there were 441 people, 166 households, and 119 families residing in the borough.

Według Biura Wyliczeniowego Stanów Zjednoczonych, dzielnica ma łączny obszar, cały jego teren.
Miasto Grampian to pozostałości rozwijającego się przemysłu węglowego.
Miasto osiągnęło tak zrównoważoną populację, że wprowadziło się do niego komunalne źródło wody.
Wodę jest przeprowadzana z Curwensville, a następnie rozproszona na kilka zbiorników wody do użytku społecznego.
Zarząd organu wodnego zajmuje się budżetem, dotacjami i zatrudnieniem licencjonowanych techników.
Właściciele Wodnych Grampian Penn Township zatrudniają jednego pełnoetatowego licencjonowanego technika do codziennej pracy.

s_text: is a city in Dickson County, Tennessee, United States.
It is part of the Nashville Metropolitan Statistical Area.
The population at the 2010 census was 14,538, with an estimate of 14,993 in 2014.
Dickson was named for Congressman William Dickson, as was Dickson County.
Dickson is located in south-central Dickson County at (36.071485, -87.374539).
It is bordered to the east by the town of Burns.
U.S. Route 70 passes through the north side of the city as Henslee Drive it leads east to Nashville and west to Huntingdon.
Interstate 40 passes through the Dickson city limits south of the center of town, with access from Exit 172 (Tennessee State Route 46).
I-40 leads east to Nashville from Exit 172 and west to Jackson.
According to the United States Census Bureau, Dickson has a total area of , of which is land and , or 0.41 , is water.

The city center sits on the Tennessee Valley Divide, with the southwest side of the city draining via the East Piney River to the Piney River, then to the Duck River, and then to the Tennessee River, while the northeast side drains via Turnbull Creek or Jones Creek to the Harpeth River and thence to the Cumberland River.

The population was 882 at the 2010 census.
Centerville is part of the Sioux Falls, South Dakota metropolitan area.
Centerville was platted in 1883.
It was named from its location between Parker and Vermillion.
The most powerful tornado of the 2003 South Dakota tornado outbreak was located around Centerville and was rated an F-4 on the Fujita scale.
It had maximum winds of 260 miles per hour and caused damage in the town of Centerville and surrounding areas.
No fatalities were reported with this tornado.

The village is home to the general headquarters (HQ) of the Velvet Ice Cream Company at the Ye Olde Mill just south of the village.
The mill is a popular regional tourist attraction with an ice cream parlor and restaurant and wildlife and picnic areas.
The village hosts the annual ice cream parade every Memorial Day weekend and the simultaneous Utica Ice Cream Festival.
Utica was named after Utica, New York.
In 1833, Utica contained three stores, two taverns, 50 houses and 194 inhabitants.

t_text: at the 2010 census.
Centerville is part of the Sioux Falls, South Dakota metropolitan area.
Centerville was platted in 1883.
It was named from its location between Parker and Vermillion.
The most powerful tornado of the 2003 South Dakota tornado outbreak was located around Centerville and was rated an F-4 on the Fujita scale.
It had maximum winds of 260 miles per hour and caused damage in the town of Centerville and surrounding areas.
No fatalities were reported with this tornado.
Centerville is located at (43.117631, -96.960644).
According to the United States Census Bureau, the city has a total area of , all of it land.
